question_title: How many distinct Russian oil refineries will be publicly reported as struck by Ukrainian drones between June 1, 2026 and June 13, 2026?
summary:
This question asks how many distinct Russian oil refineries will be reported as struck by Ukrainian drones between June 1-13, 2026, per the Wikipedia timeline article.

Key findings: (1) May 2026 saw 9 distinct refineries struck over 31 days, giving a base rate of ~3.8 per 13-day period; (2) As of June 3, 1 refinery (Ilsky) is confirmed in the window, while other June 1-3 strikes were on non-qualifying targets (St. Petersburg oil terminal, pumping stations); (3) The campaign tempo has been accelerating with Ukraine's "logistics lockdown" announcement; (4) No ceasefire is in effect; (5) Weekly strike counts in May showed very high variance (0 to 5 refineries per week), creating clustering risk.

Monte Carlo simulation using a Poisson-gamma mixture model calibrated to May 2026 data yields a median of 3 with P10-P90 of 1-7. The floor is 1 (already confirmed) and the right tail reflects possible burst weeks of 5+ refineries.

factors.description: May 2026 base rate: 9 distinct refineries struck over 31 days (0.29/day), projecting ~3.8 for 13 days
Already 1 confirmed refinery (Ilsky, June 2) in first 3 days of window, establishing hard floor
Logistics lockdown campaign announced May 27 signals sustained/accelerating energy infrastructure targeting
High overdispersion in weekly data (0 to 5 per week in May) creates genuine tail risk in both directions
Ukraine increasingly targeting non-refinery oil infrastructure (terminals, depots, pumping stations), diluting refinery-specific strike rate
Wikipedia reporting lag may miss some late-window (June 11-13) strikes not yet documented by June 13 23:59 UTC
Russian air defense improvements - 354 drones claimed intercepted June 3 - may filter out some successful strikes
No ceasefire in effect; Trump-brokered Jan 2026 energy ceasefire collapsed by late March; no diplomatic constraints

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary

**Question:** How many distinct Russian oil refineries will be publicly reported as struck by Ukrainian drones between June 1-13, 2026?

**My forecast:** Median of **3 refineries**, with a P10-P90 range of **1-7**.

### Key Evidence
- **May 2026 base rate:** 9 distinct refineries struck over 31 days (Kirishi, Ryazan, Moscow, Yaroslavl, Nizhny Novgorod, Syzran, Volgograd, Saratov, Novoshakhtinsky) = ~3.8 per 13-day window
- **Confirmed so far (June 1-3):** 1 refinery (Ilsky, struck June 2). Other June strikes were on non-qualifying targets: St. Petersburg oil *terminal* (excluded), Zenzevatka *pumping station* (excluded), Armavir *oil depot* (excluded)
- **Escalation signals:** Ukraine's "logistics lockdown" campaign (announced May 27), no ceasefire in effect, St. Petersburg Economic Forum (June 3-7) providing motivation for spectacular strikes
- **Downward pressures:** Increasing targeting of non-refinery infrastructure, Wikipedia reporting lag for late-window strikes, Russian air defense claims

### Distribution Shape
The distribution is right-skewed with a hard floor at 1 (confirmed). The high weekly variance observed in May (0 to 5 refineries per week) creates genuine fat-tail risk, with ~4% probability of 10+ refineries if Ukraine executes an intensive burst campaign.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) How many distinct Russian oil refineries will be publicly reported as struck by Ukrainian drones between June 1, 2026 and June 13, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43775)
[Ukrainian Drones Hit Ilsky Oil Refinery for 16th Time: June 2026 | News | informedclearly](https://informedclearly.com/en/war/54050/ukrainian-drones-ilsky-oil-refinery-2026)
[Ukrainian drone attacks hit multiple Russian targets, including refinery](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/5/31/ukrainian-drone-attacks-hit-multiple-russian-targets-including-refinery)
[Ukrainian drones strike St. Petersburg, hours before ‘Putin’s Davos’ opens | CNN](https://www.cnn.com/2026/06/03/europe/ukraine-drone-attack-russia-st-petersburg-intl-hnk)
[Ukrainian drones strike a St. Petersburg oil terminal ahead of Putin visit](https://www.npr.org/2026/06/03/nx-s1-5844793/ukrainian-drones-hit-st-petersburg)
[Crimea short of fuel as Ukraine expands attacks on Russian oil facilities | CNN](https://www.cnn.com/2026/06/01/europe/crimea-fuel-ukraine-russian-oil-intl)

[Drone Strikes Force Central Russian Refineries to Halt or Cut Output – Reuters - The Moscow Times](https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2026/05/20/drone-strikes-force-central-russian-refineries-to-halt-or-cut-output-reuters-a92805)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves as the number of distinct Russian oil refineries (i.e., crude oil refining plants located in the Russian Federation, excluding standalone oil storage depots, pumping stations, chemical plants, and export/marine terminals) reported as struck by Ukrainian drones on dates between June 1, 2026 and June 13, 2026 inclusive, as listed in the 'Timeline of the Russo-Ukrainian war (1 January 2026 – present)' Wikipedia article (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Timeline_of_the_Russo-Ukrainian_war_(1_January_2026_%E2%80%93_present)) as of 23:59 UTC on June 13, 2026. Each refinery is counted at most once regardless of how many times it is struck during the window. For example, if Volgograd, Ryazan, and Saratov refineries are each listed as struck in the window, the answer is 3. A value of 4 refineries would be entered as '4'. If that Wikipedia article is unavailable or omits coverage of this window, the resolver will instead compile the count from [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) such as Reuters wire reports on Ukrainian strikes on Russian refineries during the same window.
fine_print: A 'strike' means the Wikipedia entry (or fallback source) reports that Ukrainian drones hit, damaged, or set on fire the refinery or its territory/processing units. Entries reporting only intercepted drones with no impact on a refinery do not count. If a strike is reported as combining drones with cruise/ballistic missiles, the refinery still counts provided drones are described as part of the attack. Combined oil/gas processing plants (e.g., Gazprom gas processing facilities) count only if the source describes them as an oil refinery. Refineries in Russian-occupied Ukrainian territory (e.g., Crimea) count as Russian for this question. Refineries located in Belarus or other non-Russian territory do not count.

input.anchor: May 2026 empirical rate: 9 distinct refineries in 31 days = ~3.8 per 13-day period. Source: Moscow Times/Reuters reporting on May refinery strikes.
input.assessment:
The question asks about distinct Russian oil refineries (specifically crude oil refining plants) struck by Ukrainian drones between June 1-13, 2026, as reported on the Wikipedia timeline article.

Key evidence:
1. May 2026 saw 9 distinct refineries struck over 31 days (Kirishi, Ryazan, Moscow, Yaroslavl, Nizhny Novgorod, Syzran, Volgograd, Saratov, Novoshakhtinsky)
2. As of June 3, 1 refinery (Ilsky) is confirmed struck in the June 1-13 window
3. Several non-refinery targets were also hit (St. Petersburg oil terminal, Zenzevatka pumping station, Armavir oil depot) - these don't count
4. The campaign tempo has been accelerating in 2026, with the "logistics lockdown" announcement
5. No ceasefire is in effect

The Monte Carlo simulation using a Poisson-gamma mixture model calibrated to May 2026 data produces a median of 3, with P10=1 and P90=7. The negative binomial model gives similar results. The high overdispersion in weekly data (0 to 5 refineries per week in May) means there's genuine tail risk in both directions.

The distribution is right-skewed: the floor is 1 (already confirmed), but intense strike weeks could push to 7+ distinct refineries. The question specifically excludes terminals, depots, and pumping stations, which reduces the count relative to total oil infrastructure strikes.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor (3.8 per 13 days from May rate) is close to my center estimate of 3-4. The confirmed floor of 1 refinery in 3 days is consistent with the May daily rate. Distribution width (P10-P90 = 1 to 7) reflects the high observed variance in weekly strike counts. Not hedging toward center - the evidence genuinely supports asymmetric uncertainty with a right tail.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether the current striking of non-refinery targets (terminals, depots) represents a temporary shift or a sustained reallocation away from refineries. 2. The clustering pattern - May had a week with 0 refineries and a week with 5. June 4-13 could easily be in either regime. 3. Wikipedia capture rate for late-window strikes.
input.tool_audit: Useful: ISW daily assessments (June 1-3) provided excellent granular strike data; web search found specific June 2 Ilsky strike and June 3 St. Petersburg terminal strike; research() sub-agent compiled comprehensive May 2026 data. Empty/limited: Wikipedia fetch returned only the article title without the detailed timeline content. The Wikipedia timeline article text could not be extracted directly, which is the actual resolution source. Failures: None.
input.update_triggers: 1. Any announcement of a ceasefire (would reduce count sharply). 2. Reports of multiple refinery strikes in a single night during the window. 3. Evidence of significant Russian air defense improvements reducing hit rates.
input.process_reflection: The research tools worked well for gathering the strike data from multiple angles (ISW, news sources, research sub-agent). The main gap was that the Wikipedia article - the actual resolution source - returned only a brief extract rather than the full day-by-day timeline. A Wikipedia-specific content extraction tool would have been invaluable. The fetch_url tool struggled with the long Wikipedia article. The Monte Carlo simulation approach was appropriate for this discrete-count question with overdispersed data.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Checks Performed

### Hallucinated Evidence
**No hallucinations found.** All key factual claims are grounded in the trace:
- **9 distinct refineries in May 2026**: Supported by (1) Moscow Times/Reuters article listing 5 central Russian refineries (Kirishi, Ryazan, Moscow, YANOS, NORSI) struck or forced offline through May 20; (2) Euronews confirming Syzran strike May 21; (3) ISW June 2 assessment confirming Volgograd (May 29), Saratov (May 31), and Novoshakhtinsky (May 31) as damaged refineries. The Metaculus question background itself lists all these refineries.
- **Ilsky confirmed June 2**: Confirmed by informedclearly.com article and ISW June 2 assessment.
- **St. Petersburg target was a terminal, not refinery**: CNN and NPR articles both specifically call it an "oil terminal," and the agent correctly excluded it.
- **Logistics lockdown announced May 27**: Confirmed in ISW assessments and research sub-agent data.
- **Trump-brokered January 2026 ceasefire collapsed**: Confirmed by research sub-agent findings; consistent with Polymarket data showing the stricter ceasefire market at only 2.65% for June 30.
- **354 drones intercepted June 3**: Confirmed from NPR article.

One minor weakness: Yaroslavl (YANOS) is listed as "~25% capacity" in the sources rather than having a confirmed specific strike date. However, the Metaculus question background and Moscow Times article both list it among refineries where "attacks knocked out or significantly cut output," so treating it as a struck refinery is reasonable.

### Double-Counting
**Minor overlap between Factors 1 and 3** (May base rate + logistics lockdown both push toward continuation/acceleration), but they're conceptually distinct: Factor 1 is empirical rate, Factor 3 is a forward-looking escalation signal. Not flaggable double-counting.

**Factor 2 (confirmed Ilsky) and Factor 1 (base rate)**: The confirmed strike is already incorporated as the floor in the Monte Carlo simulation (starts at 1), so there's no mechanical double-counting in the model output even though it appears as a separate factor.

### Wrong-Direction Factors
All factor logit signs are correct: positive for base rate, confirmed strikes, no ceasefire, escalation signal; negative for non-refinery targeting, Wikipedia lag, and air defense.

### Contradictory Assessment
The narrative assessment (center=3, range=1-7) is consistent with the factor-implied estimates. The agent's own simulations produced medians of 3-4 across multiple models. The stated center of 3.0 is modestly below the simulation mean of ~3.8-4.0, which the agent explicitly noted and attributed to conservative adjustments for non-refinery targeting shift and Wikipedia lag. This is within acceptable bounds.

### Resolution Criteria
The agent correctly engaged with all resolution criteria: (1) identified Wikipedia timeline as primary resolution source; (2) correctly distinguished refineries from excluded categories (terminals, depots, pumping stations); (3) correctly excluded St. Petersburg terminal and Zenzevatka pumping station; (4) noted the June 13 snapshot deadline; (5) understood the "distinct" counting (once per refinery regardless of multiple strikes).

### Pre-publication Event
The 1 confirmed Ilsky strike occurred June 2, which is within the question window (June 1-13) that started before the question was published (June 4). The resolution criteria explicitly cover June 1-13, so this is legitimate (Case 1 — resolution criteria specify the window includes pre-publication dates).

### Counterargument Quality
**Strong.** The counterargument argues the forecast may be too LOW, citing the late-May burst tempo, the logistics lockdown escalation, and the possibility that Wikipedia captures additional strikes not tracked by ISW. It provides specific quantitative alternative (median of 5-6 instead of 3) and concrete conditions that would change the estimate. This is a genuine attempt to break the forecast.

### Confidence Assessment
0.40 confidence is appropriate given: high overdispersion (weekly counts of 0-5 in May), inability to extract Wikipedia article content, and the 10-day forward projection period.

### Probability Assessment
The center of 3.0 with P10-P90 of 1-7 is consistent with the evidence. The May rate projects 3.8 for 13 days; the downward adjustment to 3.0 is modest and justified by the conservative factors. Multiple simulation approaches converged on median 3-4. I would estimate similarly — perhaps center 3.5 — but the difference is not meaningful given the high variance in this process. The distribution shape (right-skewed, floor at 1, fat right tail) correctly captures the clustering dynamics.

### Tool Audit
Agent claims "no tool failures" — confirmed by 0% error rate across 36 calls. Agent correctly notes Wikipedia fetch returned only the article title — confirmed in trace (line ~72). No discrepancies.
